title: Transmission-Based Precautions
subtitle: The nurse applies isolation precautions — airborne (N95, negative-pressure room), droplet (surgical mask), and contact (gown and gloves) — on top of standard precautions.

patient.name: Isolation Precautions
patient.admitDate: The nurse selects the correct transmission-based precautions.

nursesNote: The nurse applies transmission-based precautions, which are added on top of standard precautions (hand hygiene and PPE used for every patient) when a specific spread route is known or suspected. Airborne precautions are for tiny droplet nuclei that stay suspended in the air, used for tuberculosis, measles, and varicella; the patient is in a private negative-pressure room with the door closed and staff wear an N95 respirator. Droplet precautions are for larger respiratory droplets spread at close range, used for influenza, pertussis, meningitis, mumps, and rubella; staff wear a surgical mask, and the patient is in a private room. Contact precautions are for organisms spread by direct or indirect touch, used for MRSA, VRE, C. difficile, and RSV; staff wear a gown and gloves and use dedicated equipment. For C. difficile, hands are washed with soap and water because alcohol gel does not kill the spores. PPE is applied before entering and removed carefully on exit (gloves are usually removed first and the mask last), with hand hygiene before and after. The patient stays in the room when possible, and if transport is needed the patient wears a mask. The nurse teaches the patient and visitors and ensures the correct signage is posted.
providerOrders: Apply standard precautions for every patient
Use airborne precautions with an N95 and negative-pressure room for TB, measles, varicella
Use droplet precautions with a surgical mask for flu, pertussis, meningitis
Use contact precautions with gown and gloves for MRSA, VRE, C. diff, RSV
Use soap and water hand hygiene for C. difficile

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What is true about isolation precautions?

opts.text: Standard precautions apply to every patient
Airborne precautions need an N95 respirator
Droplet precautions use a surgical mask
Contact precautions need only gloves, no gown
C. diff requires soap-and-water hand hygiene
TB requires a negative-pressure room
Measles and varicella need airborne precautions
Influenza is spread mainly by droplets
MRSA and VRE need contact precautions
Alcohol gel kills C. diff spores effectively
A patient who must leave the room wears a mask
Precautions are added on top of standard ones

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Order entering the room of a patient on contact precautions

opts.text: Perform hand hygiene
Put on the gown
Put on the gloves over the cuffs
Provide care using dedicated equipment
Remove PPE and perform hand hygiene on exit

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.sub: Which precaution does each disease need?

opts.text: Active tuberculosis
Influenza
Measles (rubeola)
C. difficile infection
Varicella (chickenpox)
MRSA wound infection

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: A patient with suspected pulmonary TB is admitted
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Place the patient in a negative-pressure room
Keep the door closed
Don an N95 before entering
Post airborne precaution signage
Have the patient wear a mask if transported
Wearing an N95 for a patient with active TB
Using only a surgical mask for measles
Wearing gown and gloves for MRSA care
Using alcohol gel after C. diff care
Performing hand hygiene before and after care
Leaving the TB room door open
Having the patient mask during transport
Reusing the same gloves between patients
